coredata.dc:description: Pesticides play an irreplaceable role in ensuring increased food production and income, but the overuse of pesticides can also pose a threat to food and environmental safety. A novel dual-emission fluorescent probe was developed for the facile and accurate detection of sulfonylurea herbicides. A Förster resonance energy transfer system, based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and thioglycolic acid-capped cadmium telluride quantum dots (TGA-CdTe QDs) as energy donor–acceptor pairs, was established using graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as a substrate and successfully applied for the micro-detection of mesosulfuron-methyl (Mes). The CDs-CdTe/C3N4 probe exhibits emission peaks at 445 and 593 nm with strong fluorescence intensity under single-wavelength excitation. Mes was shown to significantly decrease the fluorescence intensity of each emission peak via different quenching mechanisms, and the degree of quenching was proportional to Mes concentration. The linear range of fluorescence measurement was 0–90 µM, with limits of detection at 1.3 µM (445 nm) and 0.8 µM (593 nm). The dual-emission fluorescent probe exhibited excellent selectivity for Mes compared with the other eleven herbicides. The fluorescent probe has a satisfying detection performance in environmental water samples and wheat samples, and has potential application in the portable detection of pesticide residues.

The concentrations of the test samples for all of the above materials were based on Section 2.5, and the obtained 100 μL of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 was diluted to a final mixture volume of 3 mL for testing. The concentrations of the N-CDs, TGA-CdTe QDs and CdTe/C3N4 solutions to be tested were one-ninth, eight-ninths and eight-ninths of the CDs-CdTe/C3N4 solution concentration, respectively.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pesticides play an irreplaceable role in ensuring increased food production and income, but the overuse of pesticides can also pose a threat to food and environmental safety. A novel dual-emission fluorescent probe was developed for the facile and accurate detection of sulfonylurea herbicides. A Förster resonance energy transfer system, based on N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and thioglycolic acid-capped cadmium telluride quantum dots (TGA-CdTe QDs) as energy donor–acceptor pairs, was established using graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) as a substrate and successfully applied for the micro-detection of mesosulfuron-methyl (Mes). The CDs-CdTe/C3N4 probe exhibits emission peaks at 445 and 593 nm with strong fluorescence intensity under single-wavelength excitation. Mes was shown to significantly decrease the fluorescence intensity of each emission peak via different quenching mechanisms, and the degree of quenching was proportional to Mes concentration. The linear range of fluorescence measurement was 0–90 µM, with limits of detection at 1.3 µM (445 nm) and 0.8 µM (593 nm). The dual-emission fluorescent probe exhibited excellent selectivity for Mes compared with the other eleven herbicides. The fluorescent probe has a satisfying detection performance in environmental water samples and wheat samples, and has potential application in the portable detection of pesticide residues.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Modern agricultural practices rely heavily on pesticides for effective control of weeds and pests that can negatively impact the industry [1]. The application of herbicides, in particular, plays a significant role in enhancing grain yield [2]. Sulfonylurea herbicides (SUs) were first introduced by DuPont and inhibit weed growth mainly by acting on acetolactate synthase [3]. Over time, SUs have become one of the mainstream herbicides worldwide because of their outstanding herbicidal activity and low mammalian toxicity [4].

While the wide-scale adoption of SUs has contributed to agricultural development, there are negative impacts on water, food, animals, and subsequent crops because of their persistence in the environment [5]. Some laboratory exposure experiments have shown that SUs are a source of pollution in aquatic environments [6]. SUs can remain in the soil as well as contaminate groundwater, negatively affecting the survival of various organisms, environmental protection, and ecologically sustainable development [7]. Given these concerns, the maximum residue limits (MRLs) for SUs in most countries have great importance and necessitate assessment of residue levels [8].

Several approaches have been reported for the detection of sulfonylurea herbicides, including high-performance liquid chromatography with diode array detection (HPLC-DAD) [9], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [10], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [11], matrix-free laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (LDI-MS) [5], capillary electrophoresis with ultraviolet detection (CE-UV) [12], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) [13]. However, the inherent limitations of these methods, as well as the thermal instability and non-volatility of SUs, require highly trained operators, expensive instrumentation, and time-consuming pre-processing such as derivatization [14,15]. To make up for these shortcomings, there is a demand for simple, rapid, low-cost, sensitive assay methods. Fluorescence-based detection methods have been increasingly employed for the detection and analysis of pesticides in view of their high efficiency, excellent sensitivity, low cost, and ease of operation [16]. Most of the traditional fluorescence detection platforms are based on single-emission fluorescent probes, which are often accompanied by negative impacts on the detection results due to the presence of analyte-independent factors such as environmental conditions and instrument sensitivity [17]. Dual-emission fluorescent probes can improve the robustness and accuracy of sensing detection by simultaneously utilizing multi-dimensional signal outputs. In contrast, dual-emission fluorescent probes have a great potential for highly selective determination of analytes which are more suitable for qualitative or quantitative analyses [18,19].

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanosphere materials, and many synthetic strategies have been exploited to optimize their performance by doping with heteroatoms. CDs have numerous advantages, such as excellent photoluminescence properties, simple preparation and surface functionalization, high photostability, non-toxicity, and good biocompatibility [20], which has facilitated a wide range of applications in fluorescence sensors [21], photocatalysis [22], bioimaging [23] and drug delivery [24].

Quantum dots (QDs) have unique optical properties compared to conventional organic dyes, such as high quantum yields, luminescence-dependent size control, broad absorption spectra, narrow and tunable emission spectra, and long fluorescence lifetimes [25]. As a result of these excellent properties, many studies have established Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) systems based on QDs [26]. For example, Wang et al. developed a pesticide fluorescence sensor based on photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and FRET, which can specifically detect three pesticides in “turn-off-on” mode [27].

Occasionally the surface ligands on the QDs interact directly with the analytes, such as the selective recognition of the organochlorine pesticide dicofol using glutathione-coated CdS QDs by Walia et al. After controlled experiments, it was determined that the interaction of the adjacent chloride groups in dicofol with the –NH2 and –COOH groups of glutathione from the CdS surface caused the change in fluorescence intensity [28]. While this sensor is very sensitive, it is susceptible to potential interference by metal ions, and the reactivity of the –NH2 and –COOH groups makes the selectivity poor [15]. In contrast, the simultaneous bimodal response to pesticide molecules was achieved in this paper, which can greatly enhance the selectivity and accuracy of the fluorescent probe for the analytes.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a polymeric semiconductor nanosheet that can be simply and cheaply prepared. It has favorable chemical and thermal stability, high fluorescence quantum yield, good biocompatibility and non-toxicity [29]. It can be used as an outstanding substrate to anchor different types of nanoparticles by in situ growth and has attracted the attention of researchers in a wide range of fields in recent years [30]. There have been several reports of the use of g-C3N4 in the detection of pesticide residues. For instance, Li et al. constructed a dual-signal nanoprobe (UV–vis and fluorescence assay) based on silver nanoparticles modified with graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4/AgNPs) for the analysis of parathion-methyl in vegetable and water samples [31].

In the present study, a combination of in situ growth and hydrothermal treatment was employed to synthesize a dual-emission fluorescent probe (CDs-CdTe/C3N4) by loading N-CDs and TGA-CdTe QDs onto g-C3N4, establishing a platform for fluorescence monitoring of SUs. As shown in Scheme 1 , the efficient transfer of photoexcited electrons improved the fluorescence sensitivity of TGA-CdTe QDs when anchored on g-C3N4 nanosheets. FRET interaction between N-CDs and TGA-CdTe QDs resulted in strong fluorescence intensity of both emission peaks (λem1 = 445 nm, λem2 = 593 nm) after adding N-CDs for hydrothermal reaction. After the addition of Mes, the fluorescence of the emission peak due to N-CDs was weakened by a static quenching effect (SQE), and the emission peak due to CdTe/C3N4 was quenched through a combination of SQE, PET between the pesticide and CdTe/C3N4, and weakened FRET between the N-CDs and TGA-CdTe QDs. A quantitative and selective experimental analysis of the dual-signal simultaneous response of Mes was carried out by establishing a linear detection relationship. The method is sensitive and rapid for the determination of Mes, and has been successfully applied to the analysis of Mes in aqueous environments and wheat samples.

Melamine, tellurium powder (Te), thioglycolic acid (TGA), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O), citric acid (CA), gallic acid (GA), diethylenetriamine, were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical CO.,LTD. Hydrochloric acid (HCl), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. The pesticides, prometryn (Pom), mesosulfuron-methyl (Mes), pinoxaden (Pin), nicosulfuron (Nic), fenoxaprop-p-ethyl (Fen), mefenacet (Mef), acetochlor (Ace), bensulfuron methyl (Ben), propyzamide (Pro), atrazine (Atr), butachlor (But) and flufenacet (Flu) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). All other reagents of analytical reagent grade were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China), and were used without additional purification.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN) was employed to visualize the size and morphology of fluorescent materials. The size distribution and Zeta potential analyses were performed using a NanoBrook ZetaPULS Zeta Potential Analyzer (Brookhaven Instruments Ltd., USA). Elemental and functional group analyses were performed for substances using a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Therme, USA) and an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). Fluorescence spectra were taken with a LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA), and a FLS980 lifetime and steady state spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) was used to test fluorescence decay curves. UV–vis absorption spectra were examined using a Lambda 35 Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). All pH measurements were carried out using a FiveEasy Plus pH acidity meter (METTLER TOLEDO, China).

N-CDs were synthesized based on the method (one-step hydrothermal method) reported by our laboratory with minor modifications [32]. 0.5 g of GA, 0.05 g of CA, 100 µL diethylenetriamine and 30 mL of deionized water were added to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Furthermore, the mixture was sonicated for 20 min at room temperature and heated to 200 °C for 10 h. After the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, insoluble material was removed by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 15 min) and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. A dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000 Da) was further purified for 48 h to remove the unreacted substance. At last, the resultant N-CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

In the beginning, g-C3N4 was prepared by a simple thermal polycondensation method [33]. 6 g of melamine was placed in a crucible with a lid in a muffle furnace, and heated to 500 ℃ under air condition at a heating rate of 5 ℃/min for 2 h. Then it was heated to 520 ℃ and calcined for 2 h. The resulting product was ground to obtain a pale yellow powdered g-C3N4.

The CDs-CdTe/C3N4 material was produced as follows: 200 mL of deionized water was degassed with N2 for 1 h in a three-neck flask to remove dissolved oxygen. 0.46 g of CdCl2·2.5H2O, 20 mg of g-C3N4 powder and 350 μL of TGA were mixed into 200 mL of deionized water under a N2 atmosphere and thoroughly stirred for 30 min. Then, the pH was adjusted to 12 with 2 M NaOH solution, and the fresh NaHTe solution prepared with Te powder and NaBH4 was quickly added into the flask. The solution was refluxed at 90 ℃ for 30 min in the dark. Then it was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing a certain amount of N-CDs solution and hydrothermally reacted at 130 ℃ for 1 h to produce CDs-CdTe/C3N4 solution. The resulting solution was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 15 min) to remove large particles, the solution was transferred to a 1000 Da dialysis bag and kept in the dark in deionized water for 24 h. Finally, the solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4℃. When the autoclave did not contain N-CDs solution, CdTe/C3N4 solution was obtained, and TGA-CdTe QDs solution was attained without g-C3N4 and N-CDs.

To detect Mes, a series of Mes solutions of different concentrations and 100 µL of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 solution were dispersed in 10 mM phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH = 7) to a final mixed liquid volume of 3 mL. After each mixture was incubated for 10 min at room temperature, the two emission spectra were recorded at the fluorescence excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The excitation and emission slit width for all fluorometric assays were fixed at 10 nm and 15 nm, respectively. In order to evaluate the selectivity of the fluorescent probe, a series of interfering pesticides were chosen instead of Mes under the same conditions, including Fen, Pin, Nic, Pro, Mef, Ace, Pom, Ben, Atr, But, Flu. An equal concentration of each pesticide (200 μM) was used for the selectivity test by a similar procedure to that mentioned above.
For evaluating the performance of the method in practical application, CDs-CdTe/C3N4 was applied to detect Mes in water samples from the environment and in wheat samples.

Real water samples were gathered from deionized water, tap water and Fen River water. Before fluorescence measurement, the water samples were centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 15 min, the supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane to remove large particles. Next, and then PBS buffer (pH = 7, 10 mM) was prepared with different water samples as the dispersion medium.

The dried wheat samples were first broken into powder by a blender, and 1 g of wheat powder was placed in 4 mL of acetonitrile and 1 mL of deionized water for 10 min of ultrasonic treatment. Then the mixture was transferred to a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane, dried to dryness by vacuum, and redissolved in PBS buffer solution (pH = 7, 10 mM).

PBS buffer samples with different concentrations of Mes were mixed in the previously prepared system to be tested. Fluorescence emission data were collected and analyzed by the proposed method, and the recoveries were calculated.

TEM images were used to characterize the morphology and size of N-CDs, TGA-CdTe QDs and CDs-CdTe/C3N4. In Fig. 1 A, it can be seen that N-CDs are spherical and uniformly dispersed, and the histogram in the inset of Fig. 1A shows an average particle size of 4.37 nm. The morphology of TGA-CdTe QDs is similarly spherical, with an average particle size of 3.13 nm (Fig. 1B). The top right inset of Fig. 1B shows the lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.36 nm, which corresponds to the (111) characteristic crystal plane of CdTe QDs [34]. The dispersion states of the two nanoparticles indicate their good hydrophilicity. As shown in Fig. 1C, g-C3N4 presents as nanosheets with a lateral size of about 300 nm, together with N-CDs and TGA-CdTe QDs covering the surface. The aggregation of some nanoparticles can be seen in Fig. 1D, which may result from electrostatic interaction between the amino groups on the surface of N-CDs and the carboxyl groups on the surface of TGA-CdTe QDs [35].

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were utilized to investigate the interactions generated between the materials during the synthesis of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 fluorescent probe. Previous literature has shown that the surface of g-C3N4 has oxygen-containing defects and amino groups, which can be used as anchor points to fix the Cd precursors onto g-C3N4 nanosheets by in situ growth [36]. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the infrared features of both TGA-CdTe QDs and g-C3N4 are retained in the FTIR spectrum of CdTe/C3N4. The absence of a characteristic peak near 2600–2500 cm−1 due to –SH indicates that TGA successfully modified the CdTe QDs by forming Cd–S bonds [37]. Moreover, the typical respiration pattern of tri-s-triazine units in g-C3N4 appears as a sharp and intense peak at 808 cm−1 [38]. Corresponding peaks in the FTIR spectrum of CdTe/C3N4 are observed to be slightly shifted, also suggesting some interaction between TGA-CdTe QDs and g-C3N4.

As depicted in Fig. 2B, the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs contains a broad band at 3431 cm−1, corresponding to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations [39]. Moreover, three absorption peaks at 1691, 1653 and 1560 cm−1 reflect the CO stretching vibration in ester and carboxylic acid groups, amide modes I and II, respectively [40]. The features of the spectrum indicate the presence of abundant hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surface of N-CDs. Since g-C3N4 is rich in nitrogen-containing sites from tri-s-triazine units and terminal amino groups, and many carboxyl groups are present in TGA on the surface of TGA-CdTe QDs, hydrogen bonding interactions between N-CDs and CdTe/C3N4 may occur [41]. Upon closer examination of the spectra, it can be seen that the N–H or O–H peaks of N-CDs are broadened and appear red-shifted in the CDs-CdTe/C3N4 spectrum, which indicates that they are participating in hydrogen bonding. Other absorption peaks in the CDs-CdTe/C3N4 spectrum correspond to IR features of g-C3N4, TGA-CdTe QDs and N-CDs that are largely preserved and overlap.

The elemental composition and chemical state of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 were further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full spectrum diagram (Fig. 2C) confirms that the main elements present in CDs-CdTe/C3N4 are C, O, N, Cd, Te, and S, which is consistent with the analysis results of FTIR. Fig. 2D shows the high-resolution C1s spectrum, containing three distinct peaks at 284.80, 286.28 and 288.11 eV ascribed to sp2-hybridized C–C/CC, C–N/C–O and CO/sp2-hybridized carbon bond (N–CN) in nitrogen aromatic ring, respectively [36,42]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) is composed of two deconvoluted peaks with binding energies of 398.55 and 399.99 eV. The former arises from the sp2 hybridized CN–C bond of the triazine ring in g-C3N4, and the latter is due to N–(C)3/N–H [43,44]. Fig. 2F reveals that the O 1 s spectrum is deconvoluted into three peaks with the binding energies of 531.27 eV, 532.43 eV and 535.69 eV from CO, C-O/C-OH and O-CO, respectively [45]. As displayed in Fig. 2G, the Cd3d spectrum exhibits two peaks at 404.88 eV (Cd3d5/2) and 411.63 eV (Cd3d3/2), and Fig. 2H shows the Te3d spectrum peaks at 573.36 eV (Te3d5/2) and 581.12 eV (Te3d3/2), which are consistent with a Cd–Te bonding state [46]. In the S2p spectrum (Fig. 2I), two peaks at 161.81 and 163.30 eV correspond to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2, respectively, indicating the formation of complexes with TGA around the CdTe core [47]. The combination of FTIR and XPS data demonstrates that the composite CDs-CdTe/C3N4 was successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of dual-emission fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 were analyzed by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. It can be seen in Fig. 3 A that the absorption peak of N-CDs at 243 nm is attributed to π-π* transition of CC, and that at 355 nm is assigned to n-π* transition of CO as well as the typical absorption of aromatic π systems [40,48]. The peaks due to g-C3N4 and N-CDs are not evident in the absorption spectra of CDs-CdTe/C3N4, probably as a result of their low levels. In the inset of Fig. 3A, CDs-CdTe/C3N4 has a wide absorption band at about 578 nm. It is noteworthy that the absorption peaks of CdTe/C3N4 and CDs-CdTe/C3N4 are red-shifted and broadened at this point relative to pure TGA-CdTe QDs, suggesting a change in the size or surface properties of the fluorescent nanoparticles [49].

Fluorescence measurement is shown below. As we can see in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence spectrum of CdTe/C3N4 contains a pronounced emission peak near 600 nm, which is red-shifted and significantly enhanced compared with that of TGA-CdTe QDs, resulting in a more vibrant orange-red color under 365 nm UV irradiation (Fig. 3B). The observed red-shift of the emission may be due to the non-radiative transition and the difference in energy levels between the ground and excited states induced during attachment of TGA-CdTe QDs to g-C3N4 [50], which is in agreement with the UV–vis absorption data. The g-C3N4 aqueous dispersion exhibits a maximum emission peak at 438 nm, which is blue under 365 nm UV irradiation. Interestingly, the fluorescence emission due to g-C3N4 is almost absent in the fluorescence spectrum of CdTe/C3N4, which may be a consequence of the efficient transfer of photoexcited electrons from g-C3N4 to TGA-CdTe QDs [51], also resulting in enhanced fluorescence intensity at 600 nm.

As can be noticed from Fig. 3C, CDs-CdTe/C3N4 has emission peaks at 445 and 593 nm under 380 nm excitation, with pink fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. The emission peaks corresponding to N-CDs and CdTe/C3N4 are slightly shifted, with their intensities weakened and enhanced, respectively. The positions of the two emission peaks of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 remain almost constant as the excitation wavelength varies (Fig. 3D), which implies that the sizes and surface states of the sp2 clusters contained therein should be uniform [52]. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs overlapped greatly with the absorption spectrum of TGA-CdTe QDs (Fig. 3E), suggesting that FRET might have occurred [53]. FRET is heavily dependent on interparticle distance [54]. Electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding between the nanomaterials can close the distance between N-CDs and CdTe/C3N4, providing suitable conditions for FRET between them. Last but not least, by looking at Fig. 3F, it can be noticed that the fluorescence spectra of these materials are different in terms of emission position and intensity. It further illustrates that the relationship between N-CDs, TGA-CdTe QDs and g-C3N4 in the composites is not purely physical mixing, but a certain interaction through stirring and hydrothermal treatment. These phenomena demonstrate that the N-CDs and TGA-CdTe QDs were anchored on g-C3N4, and that the dual-emission fluorescent probe was successfully prepared.

To acquire higher sensitivity, the pH of the solution was optimized. As displayed in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence intensity at 593 nm of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 is quite weak at pH 1–3. It can also be observed from the inset that under UV light, CdTe/C3N4 forms an orange precipitate at pH3 and the supernatant of the CDs-CdTe/C3N4 solution is blue-green. This is due to the displacement of the weak acid TGA by the strong acid HCl with the introduction of hydrogen ions. This disrupts the Cd–S bond and conjugate acid is formed through acceptance of protons by TGA. The resulting destabilization of the nanocrystals leads to detachment of the passivated ligand (TGA) from the surface, reduction of the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs and precipitate formation [45]. In the acidic range, the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm is weak, but becomes stronger as the pH is increases. The double-peaked fluorescence intensity is maximized at pH7. In order to lower the detection limit, the pH value of the solution used in the experiment was set to 7.

The effect of different volume ratios of N-CDs and CdTe/C3N4 on the CDs-CdTe/C3N4 system during preparation were studied. An equal volume of the prepared CDs-CdTe/C3N4 solution was dispersed in PBS buffer solution (10 mM) at pH7. As shown in Fig. 4B, a volume ratio of CdTe/C3N4 to N-CDs solution of 8:1 was optimal to achieve a high intensity of both peaks.

The influence of reaction time on the detection of Mes was explored by using (F(0) − F)/F(0) as the longitudinal index, where F and F(0) are the fluorescence intensities of the fluorescent probe in the presence or absence of Mes, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, after the addition of Mes to the CDs-CdTe/C3N4 solution, the double-peaked fluorescence intensity decreased rapidly until it remained unchanged after about 10 min, and the fluorescence signal remained almost constant during 60 min. Therefore, all subsequent experiments were performed after a reaction time of 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual fluorescence signal detection of Mes
ce:section.ce:para: The dual fluorescence signal responses of the nanofluorescent probe to different concentrations of Mes were investigated under optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity at both emission peaks (445 and 593 nm) under 380 nm excitation decreased gradually as the Mes concentration was increased. Fig. 5B shows a good linear relationship between log[F(0)/F] and Mes concentration over a range of 0–90 µM. The linear regression equation at 445 nm is Y = 0.0043X + 0.0073 (R2 = 0.9991), and at 593 nm is Y = 0.0039X - 0.0061 (R2 = 0.9966), where Y is the log[F(0)/F] corresponding to the emission peak. According to the equation LOD (limit of detection) = 3σ/k, the LODs at 445 and 593 nm are 1.3 µM and 0.8 µM, respectively, where k is the slope of the calibration curve and σ is the standard deviation of the blank.

Excellent selectivity is crucial for practical applications of any kind of fluorescence sensor. To investigate the selectivity of the prepared CDs-CdTe/C3N4 to Mes, the effects of some common herbicides on the signal of this fluorescent probe were examined. Different pesticides were added to the CDs-CdTe/C3N4 solution to make a final concentration of 200 µM. As seen from Fig. 6 A and 6B, Nic had only a slight quenching effect on the fluorescence of this probe. Ben, which is structurally similar to Mes (Fig. 6D), significantly quenched the fluorescence of the emission peak at 593 nm, but had a weaker attenuating effect at 445 nm. The other herbicides had no remarkable impact on the double-peaked fluorescence intensity. In addition, Fig. 6C shows that coexisted interfering pesticides have little influence on the detection of Mes. It can be concluded that the dual-emission fluorescent probe was more sensitive to Mes than to other herbicides. Therefore, the developed probe is highly selective for the detection of Mes and is resistant to interference from other pesticides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response mechanism of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 probe to Mes
ce:section.ce:para:
The absence of overlap between the absorption spectrum of Mes and the emission spectrum of CDs-CdTe/C3N4 is displayed in Fig. 7 A, which excludes the possibility of FRET or an inner filter effect (IFE) between Mes and CDs-CdTe/C3N4 [17,55]. Analysis of the FTIR and XPS data indicate the presence of many hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surface of the CDs-CdTe/C3N4. The presence of N-H bonds in Mes enables hydrogen bonding between the two which can facilitate interactions that cause fluorescence quenching [56]. Fig. 7B exhibits a comparison of the absorption spectrum of N-CDs with that of a mixture of N-CDs and Mes. The slight blueshift of the N-CDs absorption peak at 355 nm suggests that a steady non-fluorescent ground-state complex may be formed [57]. The tested fluorescence decay curves were fitted with Eq. (1) and the average lifetime τ was calculated by Eq. (2) [58]:

As evidenced in Fig. 7C, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs were not obviously changed in the presence or absence of Mes, further indicating that the fluorescence quenching between N-CDs and Mes is essentially a SQE [59].

As observed from Fig. 7D and 7E, the fluorescence lifetimes of pure TGA-CdTe solution were not significantly different in the presence or absence of Mes, and the UV absorption spectra of CdTe/C3N4 solution before and after the addition of Mes shows no change in the absorption position but in the intensity, suggesting that there may be a static type of quenching [60,61]. Besides, As can be noticed from Fig. 7F, the zeta potential of the CdTe/C3N4 solution shifted from −30.57 to −16.32 mV after the addition of Mes, indicating a possible electron transfer effect [62]. And the fluorescence lifetime of CdTe/C3N4 in the presence of Mes changed from 25.67 to 23.06 ns (Fig. 7G). All of these phenomena indicate that the SQE and PET interactions between Mes and CdTe/C3N4 may have occurred simultaneously [35,55]. In addition, the simultaneous quenching of the fluorescence of N-CDs also attenuates FRET between N-CDs and TGA-CdTe QDs, which will also weaken the fluorescence of the peak due to CdTe/C3N4 in CDs-CdTe/C3N4.

In summary, the fluorescence of the emission peak arising from N-CDs (445 nm) in CDs-CdTe/C3N4 was weakened due to SQE after addition of Mes. The fluorescence quenching of the peak due to CdTe/C3N4 (593 nm) resulted from a combination of SQE, PET, and weakened FRET between N-CDs and CdTe/C3N4.

To assess the accuracy and feasibility of the introduced method for Mes detection in real samples, various amounts of Mes (20 µM, 50 µM and 70 µM) were spiked into the detection system of different dispersion media. Spectroscopic measurements of deionized water, tap water, Fen River water and wheat samples were carried out under the optimized conditions and five parallel assays were performed for each sample. The recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) are shown in Table 1 .

As shown in Table 1, the recoveries of Mes are 96.7 %–104.2 % (445 nm) and 97.6 %–103.3 % (593 nm), with RSDs less than 4.3 %. These results demonstrate that the dual-emission fluorescent probe is reliable for determination of Mes in real samples.

In summary, a novel environment-friendly strategy for the rapid and specific detection of Mes, based on CDs-CdTe/C3N4 fluorescent probe with dual emission signals was proposed. When the CDs-CdTe/C3N4 solution was mixed with Mes, the fluorescence of both emission peaks was quenched via different mechanisms. The degree of fluorescence quenching exhibits a simultaneous fluorescence dual-signal response related to the concentration of Mes within a certain range, and the strategy was applied to real samples with satisfactory results. Compared with the existing test methods for SUs, this method joins a new journey of rapid detection for SUs with fluorescence spectroscopy. This method has advantages that include simpleness, high selectivity, sensitivity, good precision and low cost, which holds the potential applications in the fields related to environmental protection and food safety.
